title: Is this land wasted?
summary: In a Berlin community garden, a long-time gardener and a young visitor disagree about what 'useful' land means.

body.speaker: Visitor
body.text: Excuse me. Is this a real garden? It looks a bit wild.

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: It is a real garden. I have worked here for twelve years. My name is Elena.

body.speaker: Visitor
body.text: But the grass is tall. The paths are not straight. In my city, a garden is clean.

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: Clean is one word. Alive is another. We have bees here. We have small birds. They need the tall grass.

body.speaker: Visitor
body.text: So you do not cut the grass?

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: We cut some parts. Not all parts. The bees do not eat from short grass. They eat from the small flowers in the tall grass.

body.speaker: Visitor
body.text: I see. But this land is in the middle of Berlin. It is expensive. Is it not wasted?

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: Wasted? Look around. Children play here on Saturday. Old people sit here on Sunday. A teacher brings her class on Thursday. Nothing is wasted.

body.speaker: Visitor
body.text: In my country, we build on every empty piece of land. We say the city needs houses.

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: The city needs houses. That is true. The city also needs air. And quiet. And a place where a child can see a bee and ask, 'What is that?'

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: Your grandmother sounds like a smart woman. Come on Saturday. Bring her a flower from here.

annotations.meaningVi: lãng phí / dùng không đúng giá trị

grammarNotes.title: Contrast with 'but' and parallel sentences
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong đối thoại, hai người dùng các câu ngắn song song để nêu hai quan điểm. Ví dụ: 'Clean is one word. Alive is another.' và 'The city needs houses. The city also needs air.' Cấu trúc này (lặp chủ ngữ + động từ, đổi tân ngữ) là cách A1 rất hữu hiệu để so sánh hai ý.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses short parallel sentences to set two views side by side: 'Clean is one word. Alive is another.' and 'The city needs houses. The city also needs air.' Repeating subject + verb and changing the object is a clean A1 way to compare two ideas.

translationVi:
Khách: Xin lỗi. Đây là vườn thật à? Trông hơi hoang.
Elena: Là vườn thật. Tôi làm ở đây mười hai năm rồi. Tên tôi là Elena.
Khách: Nhưng cỏ cao. Lối đi không thẳng. Ở thành phố tôi, vườn phải sạch sẽ.
Elena: Sạch là một chữ. Sống là một chữ khác. Ở đây có ong. Có chim nhỏ. Chúng cần cỏ cao.
Khách: Vậy chị không cắt cỏ à?
Elena: Có cắt vài phần. Không cắt tất cả. Ong không lấy mật từ cỏ ngắn. Chúng lấy từ những bông hoa nhỏ trong cỏ cao.
Khách: Tôi hiểu. Nhưng mảnh đất này nằm giữa Berlin. Đắt lắm. Có phải đang lãng phí không?
Elena: Lãng phí? Nhìn quanh xem. Trẻ con chơi ở đây ngày thứ Bảy. Người già ngồi ở đây ngày Chủ nhật. Một cô giáo dẫn lớp đến vào thứ Năm. Không có gì lãng phí.
Khách: Ở nước tôi, người ta xây trên mỗi mảnh đất trống. Người ta bảo thành phố cần nhà.
Elena: Thành phố cần nhà. Đúng vậy. Thành phố cũng cần không khí. Cần sự yên. Cần một nơi mà một đứa trẻ có thể thấy con ong và hỏi: 'Cái gì đấy?'
Khách: Chị nói giống bà tôi.
Elena: Bà của bạn nghe có vẻ là một người phụ nữ sáng suốt. Thứ Bảy đến đi. Mang cho bà một bông hoa từ chỗ này.

questions.explanation: 'This land is in the middle of Berlin.'

questions.prompt: How long has Elena worked there?
questions.explanation: 'I have worked here for twelve years.'

questions.prompt: Why does Elena leave some grass tall?
options: The bees eat from small flowers in tall grass
She is too tired to cut it
The city pays her to keep it tall
The grass cannot be cut by law
questions.explanation: 'The bees do not eat from short grass. They eat from the small flowers in the tall grass.'

questions.prompt: Who uses the garden on Saturday?
Who uses the garden on Sunday?
What does the visitor say happens in their own country?
options: They build on every empty piece of land
They cut all the grass weekly
questions.explanation: 'In my country, we build on every empty piece of land.'
questions.hint: Visitor's view late in the dialogue.

questions.prompt: According to Elena, what does the city also need besides houses?
options: Air, quiet, and a place where a child can see a bee
questions.explanation: 'The city also needs air. And quiet. And a place where a child can see a bee.'
questions.hint: Elena's longer reply near the end.

questions.prompt: When Elena says 'Clean is one word. Alive is another,' what does she mean?
options: A garden can be valuable in a way that is messy but full of life
She does not understand the word 'clean'
All gardens should be cut every day
questions.explanation: She contrasts two values, not rejects one.

questions.prompt: What does Elena invite the visitor to do?
options: Come on Saturday and bring his grandmother a flower
questions.explanation: 'Come on Saturday. Bring her a flower from here.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the conversation?
options: 'Useful' land is not always the same as 'tidy' land — a wild garden serves a city too
All cities should have less grass
Old gardens should be replaced with houses
questions.explanation: The two speakers argue around exactly this question.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of Elena's last reply?
questions.explanation: She praises the grandmother and offers a flower.

cloze.template:
Visitor: Excuse me. Is this a real garden? It looks a bit {{b1}}.
Elena: It is a real garden. I have worked here for twelve {{b2}}. My name is Elena.
Visitor: But the grass is tall. The paths are not {{b3}}. In my city, a garden is clean.
Elena: Clean is one word. {{b4}} is another. We have bees here. We have small birds. They need the tall grass.
Visitor: So you do not cut the grass?
Elena: We cut some parts. Not all parts. The bees do not eat from short grass. They eat from the small flowers in the tall {{b5}}.
Visitor: I see. But this land is in the {{b6}} of Berlin. It is expensive. Is it not wasted?
Elena: Wasted? Look around. Children play here on {{b7}}. Old people sit here on Sunday. A teacher brings her class on Thursday. Nothing is wasted.
Visitor: In my country, we {{b8}} on every empty piece of land. We say the city needs houses.
Elena: The city needs houses. That is true. The city also needs {{b9}}. And quiet. And a place where a child can see a bee and ask, 'What is that?'
Visitor: You sound like my grandmother.
Elena: Your grandmother sounds like a smart woman. Come on Saturday. Bring her a {{b10}} from here.

blanks.explanation: The visitor calls it 'a bit wild'.
She has worked there for twelve years.
Small flowers in the tall grass.
We build on every empty piece of land.
Air, quiet, and a child's question.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Elena says a city also needs 'a place where a child can see a bee and ask, What is that?' Is there such a place in the neighbourhood where you live? If there is not, what small piece of land near you could become one — and who would have to agree?
